title: Tell me about yourself.
answer: Structure your answer in three parts: a one-line professional identity (your degree and focus area), 2-3 relevant highlights (projects, internships, skills), and a closing line connecting to why you're a fit for this role. Keep it under 90 seconds and avoid reciting your resume line by line.
example: "I'm a final-year Computer Science student with a focus on web development. I've built a full-stack e-commerce app using React and Node.js, and interned for two months building internal dashboards at a startup. I'm now looking to join a team where I can apply these skills on real, large-scale projects — which is what drew me to this role."

title: Walk me through your resume.
answer: Go chronologically but briefly — education, key projects, internships, certifications — spending most time on whatever is most relevant to the job, and pause naturally so the interviewer can dig into whatever interests them.

title: What are your greatest strengths?
answer: Pick 1-2 strengths genuinely relevant to the role and back each with a brief, specific example rather than just naming a trait.
example: "One strength is persistence in debugging — during my final project, I spent a full night tracing a memory leak that three of us had given up on, and finally isolated it to an unclosed database connection."

title: What is your biggest weakness?
answer: Share a real, minor weakness (not a disguised strength like 'I work too hard') along with a concrete action you're taking to improve it, showing self-awareness and growth mindset.
example: "I tend to over-engineer solutions early on. I've started timeboxing my initial design phase and asking a peer to review my approach before I start coding, which has helped me ship faster."

title: Why do you want to work for this company?
answer: Research 2-3 specific, genuine reasons — their tech stack, recent projects, culture, or growth path — and connect them to your own goals, avoiding generic statements that could apply to any company.

title: Where do you see yourself in five years?
answer: Show ambition balanced with realism and loyalty — describe growing technically and taking on more responsibility within the company or industry, rather than naming an unrelated dream job that signals you won't stay long.
example: "In five years, I'd like to have grown from a developer role into someone who mentors newer engineers and owns a significant piece of a product, ideally within a company like this one where I can keep learning."

title: What are your salary expectations?
answer: For freshers, it's usually safe to express openness to the standard package for the role while noting you've researched typical industry ranges, rather than naming an arbitrary number that could undersell or overreach.
example: "I'm looking for a package in line with the industry standard for this role, and I'm confident that will be fair given what I bring. I'm most focused right now on finding a role with strong learning opportunities."

title: How do you handle salary negotiation once you receive an offer?
answer: Express gratitude for the offer first, then, if you have a researched benchmark or competing offer, state it factually and ask if there's flexibility, rather than making ultimatums. Always negotiate respectfully and be prepared to accept a firm 'no' gracefully.
tips: Research market rates on sites like Glassdoor or LinkedIn Salary before any negotiation conversation, so your ask is grounded in data.

title: Are you open to relocation?
answer: Answer truthfully based on your actual flexibility. If open, say so clearly and positively; if there are real constraints, mention them concisely rather than agreeing just to get the offer and backing out later.

title: Describe a challenge you faced and how you overcame it.
answer: Use the STAR method — Situation, Task, Action, Result — and pick a real, specific example, ideally technical or academic, that shows a clear problem, your concrete action, and a measurable outcome.
example: "During our hackathon (Situation), our team's API integration broke six hours before the deadline (Task). I isolated the issue to a rate-limiting problem and implemented request batching (Action), and we submitted on time and placed in the top 5 (Result)."

title: Tell me about a time you failed. What did you learn?
answer: Choose a genuine failure with real stakes, own it without over-blaming external factors, and focus most of your answer on the specific lesson and how you've applied it since.

title: Describe a time you disagreed with a teammate. How did you handle it?
answer: Show that you can disagree respectfully and focus on the work, not the person — describe how you presented your reasoning, listened to theirs, and reached a resolution (even if it meant accepting their approach).

title: How do you handle criticism or negative feedback?
answer: Emphasize that you view feedback as information for improvement, not a personal attack, and give a specific example where feedback changed how you approached something afterward.

title: I notice a gap in your academic timeline. Can you explain it?
answer: Be honest and concise. Briefly state the reason (health, family, exam preparation, upskilling) and pivot quickly to what you did during that time or what you learned, keeping the focus forward-looking rather than dwelling on the gap itself.
example: "I took a year to prepare for a specific certification and also freelanced on small web projects, which actually strengthened my practical coding skills before I resumed my degree."

title: Why did you switch your specialization or branch of study?
answer: Frame the switch as an informed, positive decision rather than a mistake — explain what you discovered about your interests and how the new path better aligns with your career goals.

title: You have no work experience — why should we hire you?
answer: Redirect to concrete, transferable evidence: academic projects, internships, hackathons, open-source contributions, or freelance work, and emphasize your ability to learn quickly, which matters most for fresher roles.
example: "While I haven't worked full-time yet, I've built and deployed three full-stack projects independently and interned for two months, which gave me real exposure to production code, version control, and team workflows."

title: What do you know about our company?
answer: Research the company's core products/services, recent news, and mission before the interview, and mention 2-3 specific, accurate points rather than generic praise — this signals genuine interest over a mass job application.

title: Are you applying to other companies as well?
answer: Be honest but tactful — you can acknowledge you're exploring a few opportunities in your field without naming direct competitors in detail, while reaffirming genuine interest in this specific role.

title: Do you have any questions for us?
answer: Always have 2-3 thoughtful questions ready — about the team structure, onboarding process, or what success looks like in the first six months — since asking nothing can read as disengagement.
example: "What does success look like for someone in this role during their first six months?"

title: Is there anything else you'd like to add?
answer: Use this as a chance to reinforce one key strength you haven't had a chance to mention yet, or briefly restate your enthusiasm for the role — keep it short and don't introduce new, unrelated information.

title: What's a common mistake freshers make when answering 'Tell me about yourself'?
answer: The most common mistake is reciting the entire resume verbatim or listing unrelated personal details. Instead, keep it focused, relevant to the role, and under two minutes, ending with a clear connection to why you're interviewing.

title: What's a common mistake candidates make during salary discussions?
answer: Naming a number either far too low (undervaluing yourself) or far too high without research (seeming out of touch), and negotiating before actually having an offer in hand. Research market rates and let the employer share their range first when possible.
